abstract: A pointer to a customized read function that secure transport calls to read data from the connection.
overview:
## Return Value

Your callback must return an appropriate result code. See [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/secure-transport-result-codes].

## Discussion

Before using the secure transport API, you must create a read function (conforming to the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLReadFunc] prototype) and a write function (conforming to  the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLWriteFunc] prototype) and provide them to the library by calling the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLSetIOFuncs(_:_:_:)] function.

You may configure the underlying connection to operate in a non-blocking manner; in that case, a read operation may well return [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/errSSLWouldBlock], indicating less data than requested was transferred and nothing is wrong except that the requested I/O hasn’t completed. This result is returned to the caller from the functions [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLRead(_:_:_:_:)], [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLWrite(_:_:_:_:)], or [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLHandshake(_:)].

# SSLReadFunc

**Type Alias**

A pointer to a customized read function that secure transport calls to read data from the connection.

## Declaration

```swift
typealias SSLReadFunc = (SSLConnectionRef, UnsafeMutableRawPointer, UnsafeMutablePointer<Int>) -> OSStatus
```

## Parameters

- **connection**: A connection reference.
- **data**: On return, your callback should overwrite the memory at this location with the data read from the connection.
- **dataLength**: On input, a pointer to an integer representing the length of the data in bytes. On return, your callback should overwrite that integer with the number of bytes actually transferred.

## Return Value

Your callback must return an appropriate result code. See [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/secure-transport-result-codes].

## Discussion

Before using the secure transport API, you must create a read function (conforming to the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLReadFunc] prototype) and a write function (conforming to  the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLWriteFunc] prototype) and provide them to the library by calling the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLSetIOFuncs(_:_:_:)] function.

You may configure the underlying connection to operate in a non-blocking manner; in that case, a read operation may well return [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/errSSLWouldBlock], indicating less data than requested was transferred and nothing is wrong except that the requested I/O hasn’t completed. This result is returned to the caller from the functions [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLRead(_:_:_:_:)], [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLWrite(_:_:_:_:)], or [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SSLHandshake(_:)].
title: SSLReadFunc
